nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; open hard-bottom substrates with algae and sparse coral, typically in zones of moderate to strong current at roughly 15–40 m depth

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, krill, chopped seafood, and high-quality marine flake or pellet foods, often supplemented with marine algae or Spirulina. In the wild, has been documented feeding on small zooplankton and crustaceans near the reef. Best fed 2–3 small meals per day.

avoidWith: other Cirrhilabrus and Paracheilinus species in smaller tanks

summary: Tonozuka's Fairy Wrasse is a small reef fish from Indonesia, the Philippines, and Palau, with males displaying ruby-red and tangerine coloration and an elongated dorsal filament. Generally considered reef-safe, it suits intermediate reef aquariums with a secure, tight-fitting lid.
careNotes: Tonozuka's Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus tonozukai) was described by Allen & Kuiter in 1999; the species name honors Takamasa 'Tono' Tonozuka, a Japanese underwater photographer and long-term Bali resident. In the wild it inhabits open hard-bottom substrates with algae and sparse coral across Indonesia, the Philippines, and Palau, typically in moderate-to-strong current zones at 15–40 m depth. Males display warm shades of ruby and red with tangerine and sapphire speckles, an elongated dorsal filament, and a red anal fin; females are pink with a black caudal eyespot. A minimum aquarium volume of around 250 L (roughly 66 US gallons) with at least 120 cm of tank length is broadly recommended, allowing room for the species' active mid-water swimming style and open swimming lanes. Like most Cirrhilabrus fairy wrasses, this species is known to jump, and a tight-fitting canopy is strongly advised. Temperament is semi-aggressive, with conflict directed primarily at conspecific males and other wrasses in the related genera Cirrhilabrus and Paracheilinus; in a mixed reef with dissimilar tankmates it tends to be more peaceful. It may bully smaller flasher wrasses and has been reported to predate small ornamental shrimp. The species is best kept as a single male or as one male introduced alongside one or more females at the same time. Like other fairy wrasses, it wraps itself in a self-secreted mucus cocoon at night, which is thought to mask its scent from nocturnal predators. IUCN status: Data Deficient (assessed 2009).
breedingNotes: Captive spawning has not been reliably reported and this species is not commercially bred. In the wild, the species shows distinct pairing during spawning, consistent with other Cirrhilabrus fairy wrasses that are protogynous sequential hermaphrodites — meaning females can transition to male as social conditions allow — and that typically live in harem groups. Replicating harem social conditions (one male kept with multiple females) in a larger reef system may encourage nuptial display behavior, though documented captive reproduction remains absent.

primary.name: JL Aquatics
primary.notes: Used as primary after the supplied LiveAquaria URL (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/tonozukas-fairy-wrasse) returned HTTP 404. JL Aquatics product page provided max size (~3 in / ~7.5 cm), minimum tank (50 gal / ~190 L), temperament (moderately aggressive toward other fairy wrasses), reef-safe status, diet (meaty foods including mysis, krill, chopped seafood plus marine algae/Spirulina 2–3×/day), pair/harem social structure, predation risk on small shrimp, nocturnal mucus cocoon behavior, and jumping warning.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-verified taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), authorship (Allen & Kuiter 1999), maximum size (7.5 cm TL), native range (Western Central Pacific: Indonesia), depth (15–40 m), habitat (open hard-bottom with algae and sparse coral, current-swept), trophic level (3.3), distinct pairing during breeding, sexual dimorphism (males with elongated dorsal filament and red anal fin), and the etymology honoring photographer 'Tono' Tonozuka. IUCN: Data Deficient (2009).

additional.name: Blue Zoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (3 in), care level (intermediate), reef-safe (yes), diet (carnivore), minimum tank (50 gal), Indo-Pacific origin, and the standard wrasse jumping warning. Sexually-dimorphic pricing on the page (male specimens priced higher) is consistent with the male/female color difference reported elsewhere.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: European reef database: max size ~7 cm, family Labridae, temperature 24–26°C, depth 15–40 m, native range Indonesia/Palau/Philippines, recommended minimum aquarium volume 500 L — substantially larger than U.S. trade-page recommendations (50 gal / ~190 L). Final tank-size figure (250 L / ~66 gal with 120 cm length) adopted as a middle-ground value reflecting the source spread.
